summary: Yegge argues that traditional business requirements gathering is fundamentally flawed because it results in building products nobody actually wants. He contends that successful companies build products their creators personally want to use, citing Warren Buffett and Peter Lynch's 'invest in what you know' philosophy as the business equivalent. Using examples from his time at Geoworks building the failed HP OmniGo and contrasting with the Flip camcorder's success, he demonstrates that trimming requirements leads to better products than gathering them. The core message: if you don't already know exactly what to build, you're in the wrong business.
key_points: Requirements gathering is a leading indicator of project failure because if you need to ask customers what they want, you've already lost
The best products come from building what you personally want and would use, not from focus groups or customer interviews
Simplification trumps feature expansion - successful products often come from taking things away, not adding them
Even great products can fail due to unpredictable forces like fashion, as illustrated by the mountain bike seat extender story
The Flip camcorder succeeded by ruthlessly trimming features to essentially one button and pretty colors
money_quotes: If you're planning to build something for someone else, let someone else build it.
If it's something you want, then you already know what the requirements are. You don't need to 'gather' them.
The GBR phase of a project is a leading indicator that the project will fail.
The easiest way to build a product that kicks ass is to start with someone else's great idea and take stuff away.
When you're trimming the business requirements, then you're exhibiting healthy project behavior. This contrasts directly with gathering requirements.
Don't gather business requirements: hire domain experts.
If you don't already know exactly what to build, then you're in the wrong business.
key_insight: The need to gather business requirements is itself evidence that you don't understand your product well enough to build it successfully.
